0: <title>Environmental life cycle assessment (LCA) of Swedish semi-hard cheese </title>

a Environmental Systems Analysis, Chalmers University of Technology, SE-412 96 Göteborg, Sweden b SIK AB, The Swedish Institute for Food and Biotechnology, P.O. Box 5401, SE-402 29 Göteborg, Sweden Received 15 February 2002; accepted 19 July 2002
1: An environmental life cycle assessment was performed to investigate the environmental consequences of the life cycle of Hushallsost, a semi-hard cheese. The assessment identified those activities that contribute most to the cheese's environmental impact throughout its life cycle from extraction of ingredients to waste management. Milk production at the farm was identified as having the greatest environmental impact, followed by cheesemaking at the dairy, retailing, and the production of plastic wrapping. The environmental impact could be reduced by minimising wastage of milk and cheese throughout the life cycle, without any effect on the quality of the product. Increasing the yield of cheese would also bring about substantial improvements as less milk would have to be produced on farms. 2002 Elsevier Science Ltd. All rights reserved. Keywords: Environment; Life cycle assessment; LCA; Cheese
2: Food is a basic need, but SO is a sustainable society. There is an urgent need to increase our knowledge of the environmental consequences of food production in order to be able to make improvements that promote sustainability.

Milk and other dairy products are important ingre- dients in the Swedish diet. The Swedish National Food Administration recommends daily consumption of 0.5L milk or a corresponding amount of other milk products. In recent years there has been a trend towards increasing consumption of cheese and decreasing consumption of milk. Between 1960 and 1999, the consumption of cheese in Sweden more than doubled (Swedish Board of Agriculture, 2000).

This study arose out of the Food Chain 21 research project. One of the objectives of this project is to investigate the environmental consequences of various scenarios for the Swedish supply system for dairy

products. No information was available about the environmental consequences of Swedish cheese produc- tion, and SO it was decided to undertake this study. Arla Foods, a dairy company located in Falkenberg, Sweden, accepted an invitation to participate in the study.

It was decided that the cheesemaking dairy would be the focus of the study, and that the specific objective would be to gain knowledge about the environmental consequences of the life cycle of cheese. Also, the identification of the most environmentally important activities within the life cycle of cheese was within the scope as well as to get the collection of environmental data relating to each activity. The method chosen for the study was environmental life cycle assessment (LCA).

There are thousands of different types of cheese, and SO a choice had to be made about what kind of cheese to focus on. Cheeses are often classified according to their structure (texture and body), flavour and appearance, and grouped into the categories of hard, semi-hard, semi-soft, soft and fresh cheese (Bylund, 1995; Tetra Pak, 1999). As 80% of the cheese consumed in Sweden falls in the hard and semi-hard categories (Swedish Board of Agriculture, 2000), it was decided to choose one of these cheeses. One of the most popular of the semi-hard cheeses in Sweden is Hushallsost, a cheese

with small irregular holes and a mild, slightly sour flavour. The main product of Arla Food in Falkenberg is a Hushallsost called Angsgärden. The popularity of this particular type of cheese and the fact that it was produced by the cooperating dairy led to Angsgärden being selected for study.

LCAs have been performed for a number of dairy products. Nilsson and Lorentzon (2000) studied the environmental consequences of the processing of milk. Hogaas Eide (2002a) performed an LCA of milk in which three milk-producing dairies were investigated. A screening LCA of milk powder was undertaken by Blonk, Lafleur, and van Zeijts (1997). Lorentzon, Olsson, Reimers, and Stadig (1997) studied the environ- mental effects of coffee cream from processing at the dairy to the purchaser. An LCA of the production of soured milk was produced by Grotan (1996), while butter was the product scrutinised in the LCA pilot study of an Italian dairy company by Masoni, Sára, Scartozzi, Tarantini, and Raggi (1998). One soft cheese, a Camembert, has been studied in an LCA by Bernhard and Moos (1998). Although the system boundaries differed in these studies, one consistent finding in all the studies that included a farming component was that agriculture had the greatest environmental impact.

6: There are a number of stages in the process of producing hard and semi-hard cheese in a dairy. After being received at the dairy, the raw milk is pretreated before the actual cheesemaking begins. The pretreat- ment includes pasteurisation, followed by separation and standardisation.

Next, rennet, starter culture, calcium chloride and saltpetre are mixed with the milk. Rennet includes the enzyme chymosine, which makes the casein in milk coagulate. After cutting and whey separation the coagulated milk is called curd. Important factors during coagulation are the temperature, acidity and calcium content of the milk. The main task of the starter culture is to develop acid in the curd and to give the cheese its character. Calcium chloride controls the coagulation time of the milk while saltpetre prevents fermentation problems.

The curd is pressed and put into moulds to separate it from the liquid whey. The cheese is then either put into a container with brine or salt is added directly to the curd to improve the consistency and taste of the cheese. After the brining, the cheese is dried and packed in wax or plastic wrapping. It is then stored in a temperature- controlled room to develop the right flavour (Bylund, 1995; Ostfrämjandet, 2000). On average, Hushallsost is stored for 60 days (Johansson, 2001).

8: <header>3. Methods </header>
3.1. LCA methodology

LCA is a method of evaluating the environmental impact associated with a product, process or activity during its life cycle by identifying and quantitatively or qualitatively describing its requirements for energy and material, and the emissions and waste released to the environment. The entire life cycle is included in the assessment, which means that the product under study is followed from the initial extracting and processing of raw materials through manufacturing, distribution, and use, up to final disposal, including all transportation involved. Besides identifying the environmental impact of the product or activity, LCA also identifies which activities in the product life cycle contribute most to this impact.

LCA is an ISO standardised method (ISO 14040- 14043). An illustration of the working procedure included in the standard is shown in Fig. 1. An LCA starts with a clear statement of the goal and scope of the study, the functional unit and allocation methods, the system boundaries, the assumptions and limitations, and the impact categories chosen. The functional unit is quantitative and corresponds to a reference flow to which all other flows in the LCA are related. Allocation is the method used to determine the environmental load of the products or function of interest when several products or functions share the same process. Examples of impact categories are acidification, eutrophication and global warming.
In the inventory analysis a flow model of the technical system is constructed using data about inputs and outputs of resources, energy requirements, transport,

and emissions to air and water for all activities within the system boundaries.

The inventory analysis is followed by impact assess- ment, in which the data are interpreted in terms of their environmental impact, and the classification stage, in which the result parameters are sorted and assigned to specific impact categories. Next comes characterisation, where the result parameters are multiplied by equiv- alency factors for each specific parameter and impact category. Thereafter all parameters included in the impact category are added and the result of the impact category is obtained.

For many LCAs, including this one, characterisation is the last step. However, the goal of some studies may involve the further step of normalisation, in which the results of the impact categories from the study are compared with the total impact in the region. During weighting, the different environmental impacts are weighted against each other to get one figure for the total environmental impact.
More information about LCA can be found in Baumann and Tillmann (2002), Lindfors et al. (1995) and ISO 14040-43 (1997, 1998, 2000a, b).

11: The introduction to this paper stated that our goal was to assess the environmental impact of the life cycle of semi-hard cheese. The semi-hard cheese selected for the study was the Hushallsost Angsgärden with 26% fat content, produced by the Falkenberg dairy for Arla Foods. The study focused on the cheesemaking dairy and therefore also on the activities the dairy is able to control. The functional unit (FU) selected is 1kg of Angsgärden cheese wrapped in plastic. It is assumed that the kg of Angsgärden under study originates from the consumer package of 1.1 kg.
12: The system can be defined as everything involved in the production of Ängsgärden semi-hard cheese, includ- ing the production of the ingredients for the cheese. It includes the whole life cycle from extraction of the ingredients and materials required for cheesemaking to waste management (Fig. 2). For each ingredient and material, data on the required resources such as materials and energy have been collected as well as data on the amount of waste and emissions to water and air. The mode and distance of all transportation within the system were also taken into account. However, the study did not take account of capital goods, such as buildings and machinery, and personnel. A system expansion was performed for the waste management. A description of the system expansion and each phase shown in Fig. 2 is included in the inventory analysis.
13: The purpose of this study was to investigate the environmental consequences of Swedish cheese produc- tion in general. The data most suitable for that purpose would be average data that reflect the types of technologies used, originate from the right geographic area (Sweden) and are not too old. These requirements dictated the choice of data in this study. When it has not been possible to meet these requirements, site-specific data have been used that are as close as possible to the average situation. For example, the particular cheese under study is one of the most popular cheeses in Sweden and the dairy selected is the largest cheese producer in Sweden. Table 1 lists the main sources of data, their age, and their geographic origin. A more detailed description of the data can be found in Berlin (2001).

If neither of the above approaches is applicable, an allocation method can be used to partition the environ- mental loads between the products or functions of the shared processes. The partitioning can be based on physical correlation, such that any quantitative changes in the produced products or their functions correlate with changes in the in- and outflows of the system.

Partitioning can also be based on economic allocation, that is, on the value of the produced products as reflected in their relative prices or their gross sales value. Baumann and Tillmann (2002) give the example of a multi-output process producing gold, a valuable pro- duct, and zinc, a less valuable one. In such a situation it can be argued that economic allocation is preferable since production of the valuable product is the reason for production in the first place. Economic allocation is commonly used in relation to food products.

The cheesemaking dairy is a typical multiple-output system where allocation is needed. It produces four kinds of cheese as well as cream, whey, whey powder and milk powder. The allocation method used at the cheesemaking dairy is economic allocation, using the dairy's prices for the products to partition the in- and outflows. The allocation factor for the cheese was 67.8%. This allocation factor was not only applied to the environmental impacts of the dairy itself (emissions, waste) but also to some of the upstream processes such as the production of milk, water, nitric acid and sodium hydroxide.

The partitioning can be based on physical correlation, such that any quantitative changes in the produced products or their functions correlate with changes in the in- and outflows of the system.

Partitioning can also be based on economic allocation, that is, on the value of the produced products as reflected in their relative prices or their gross sales value. Baumann and Tillmann (2002) give the example of a multi-output process producing gold, a valuable pro- duct, and zinc, a less valuable one. In such a situation it can be argued that economic allocation is preferable since production of the valuable product is the reason for production in the first place. Economic allocation is commonly used in relation to food products.

The cheesemaking dairy is a typical multiple-output system where allocation is needed. It produces four kinds of cheese as well as cream, whey, whey powder and milk powder. The allocation method used at the cheesemaking dairy is economic allocation, using the dairy's prices for the products to partition the in- and outflows. The allocation factor for the cheese was 67.8%. This allocation factor was not only applied to the environmental impacts of the dairy itself (emissions, waste) but also to some of the upstream processes such as the production of milk, water, nitric acid and sodium hydroxide.

18: <header>3.2.4. Environmental impact categories </header>

Impact categories that are presented quantitatively are the use of resources, energy consumption, global warming (100 years), acidification, eutrophication and photochemical ozone creation potentials. The impacts on ozone depletion potential and eco-toxicity are presented qualitatively. The categories specified include the key parameters for the environmental impact of food production identified by Mattsson (1999). The key inventory parameters are nitrous oxide, methane,
ammonia and energy-related emissions. For a descrip- tion of the environmental categories used, see Hauschild and Wenzel (1998).
19: A brief description of the inventory data follows. A detailed description of the inventory data with original references can be found in Berlin (2001). The following ingredients and materials are required to produce 1 kg of Angsgärden cheese at the cheesemaking dairy: milk 10.1 kg, rennet 3 g, calcium chloride (100%) 1 g, salt- petre 0.65g salt 15 g, water 1.2kg, nitric acid (62%) 7.4g, sodium hydroxide (49%) 10.8g, plastics 9.6g and cardboard 25.6g.

The main ingredient in cheese is milk. The data used for milk production, i.e. the agricultural phase were taken from Cederberg's (1998) study of milk produc- tion. The activities included in Cederberg's study are illustrated in Fig. 3. The functional unit was 1000 kg energy-corrected milk (ECM) leaving the farm gate. ECM is a correction factor which considers both the fat and protein content of the milk. A conventional farm situated in the southwest of Sweden was the farm under study. The farm not only produced milk; meat was also produced at the same time. Therefore, an economical allocation was used where the profits from the produced products (meat and milk) were considered.

The rennet-producing company purchases stomachs from all over the world. The production of the stomachs was not included in the study, as they may be con- sidered as waste during milk and meat production. However, the transportation of the stomachs to the
Production of fertilisers, diesel, pesticides, seeds
Fig. 3. A flow diagram of activities included in the LCA of milk production (Cederberg & Mattsson, 2000).
rennet-producing company, the production of the rennet and the transportation of the rennet to the cheesemak- ing dairy were included.

Production of CaCl2 requires salt, lime and waste liquid from the production of dicalcium phosphate (DCP). The waste liquid from the production of DCP contains 13% CaCl2. This waste liquid is considered as a co-product and is used during manufacturing of CaCl2. An allocation based on weight between DCP and its waste liquid has been done. The inputs to DCP production are raw phosphate, limestone, hydrochloric acid (HCI) and energy. HCI is produced at the same factory as DCP and CaCl2. It is a co-product during the production of potassium sulphate (K2SO4). Allocation based on weight was made between K2SO4 and HCI.

The saltpetre (KNO3) used in the cheesemaking dairy was produced in Sweden using potassium chloride and nitric acid. The potassium chloride originated in Germany and the nitric acid was produced at the same factory as the saltpetre. During the production of saltpetre, hydrochloric acid was produced as a co- product. Therefore a weight allocation was performed between saltpetre and hydrochloric acid.

The salt used by the Falkenberg dairy was extracted in Denmark. The method used to produce the salt includes brine pumping, drying and purification. The water used at the dairy is ground water treated in a water treatment plant to the quality of drinking water. Energy and chemicals are required to disinfect the water, and energy is also used during its distribution.

The cleaning of dairy equipment requires both acidic and alkaline detergents, in this case, nitric acid and sodium hydroxide. The nitric acid was produced in Sweden from ammonia and air. The production of ammonia was assumed to take place in Russia, using natural gas and energy as inputs. Sodium hydroxide is produced in Sweden and the resources required are iron, limestone, water, sodium chloride, sand and energy.
The cheese was wrapped in a plastic coating at the dairy. Two different kinds of plastic foils both made of low-density polyethylene (LDPE) and polyamide (PA) were used.

In 1999 the cheese dairy of Arla Foods in Falkenberg, Sweden, used 254000 tonnes of incoming milk to produce 19700 tonnes of cheese, 3900 tonnes of milk powder, 52 200 tonnes of different whey concentrates and powder, and 13 000 tonnes of cream (Arla Foods AB, 2000). Economic allocation was performed, as described in Section 3.2. All wastewater effluent includ- ing water emissions from the cheesemaking dairy was treated in the municipal sewage treatment plant.
The starter culture was produced at the dairy and is included in the total figures for the cheesemaking dairy.
During transportation of the cheese from the cheese- making dairy to the retailer, a cardboard box of corrugated board was used as secondary packaging.

The corrugated board consists of kraftliner and semi- chemical fluting. The layers are glued together with cornstarch. The cornstarch was not included in this LCA as a very small amount was used. Energy requirements for forestry and hauling were taken into account in relation to the processing of wood.

At the retailer the cheese was stored in a cold room or a refrigerator, and electricity was required for cooling. Consumers purchasing the cheese used various modes of transportation, including cars. Swedish statistical data on the distance travelled for food purchasing and the modes of transportation used by consumers in Sweden were used. Because customers generally purchase other products at the same time as they purchase cheese, an economic allocation was performed between Angsgarden cheese and other food products purchased at the same time, using statistical data on the average purchase in a Swedish food store and the price of Angsgärden.

The energy requirements of the cheese once in a household were calculated based on the average refrigerator's electricity consumption, the space required for the cheese in the refrigerator, the consumption of hard and semi-hard cheese in Sweden, and the number of persons in an average household. It was assumed that every household always has a cheese in the refrigerator.

Waste generated at the household included both the plastic wrapping and some of the cheese (3%), but not the household wastewater (after consumption). The cardboard box used to deliver the cheese to the retailer was also treated as waste. Statistics show that 45% of the waste generated in Swedish households was incin- erated in 1998 (SEPA, 2002), and this figure will probably increase when a ban on using landfills to dispose of organic waste comes into effect in 2005 (Miljödepartementet, 2001). In this study all waste was considered to be incinerated except part of the card- board, which was assumed to be recycled. Energy was recovered during incineration and therefore a system expansion was done, as illustrated in Fig. 4. The system was compensated for the delivered energy by subtracting the resource use and emissions from alternative energy production. In Sweden, the recycling rate of corrugated board is 84% (Returwell, 2001), with the remainder being incinerated. Of the recycled corrugated board, 44% was assumed to replace new fibres while the remainder replaces recycled fibres (Ekvall, 2000). How- ever, the replacement of recycled fibre causes a surplus of recycled fibres on the market, and therefore the same amount of recycled fibre is assumed to leave the market as waste. Fig. 5 illustrates the system expansion for corrugated board.

The sources of energy used in this LCA are natural gas, oil and electricity. The Swedish mixture of energy sources for electricity production was used when the requirement for electricity was located in Sweden, and

Fig. 4. A system expansion was performed (the bold part of the figure) as energy was recovered during incineration. The system was compensated for the delivered energy by subtracting it from the resource use and emissions from another source of energy.

Fig. 5. The waste of corrugated board is partly incinerated and partly recycled. A system expansion was performed (the bold part of the figure) as the recycled corrugated board replaces paper produced from virgin material as well as paper produced from already recycled material. The effect of this is a decreased production of paper from virgin material and the replaced recycled paper is incinerated.

the European mixture was used when the requirements was located in Europe. Extraction, production and combustion, as well as the transportation of the energy sources, were included in the LCA. The electricity used in Sweden was considered to have a grid loss of 5%. The Swedish electricity used in households was assumed to have grid losses of 10% (Brännström-Norberg, Deth- lefsen, Johansson, Setterwall, & Tunbrant, 1996). The energy sources used for the Swedish electricity mixture were hydropower 46.8%, nuclear power 46.55%, oil condense power 2.70%, combined power/heat, renew- able fuels 1.70%, coal condense power 1.55%, combined power/heat, natural gas 0.50%, gas turbines 0.10% and wind power 0.10% (Brännstrom-Norberg et al., 1996). European electricity was considered to have grid losses of 9% (Frees & Weidema, 1998). The distribution of fuels used to generate European electricity in 1994 was hard coal 35.5%, nuclear power 18%, lignite 15.5%, fuel oil (including diesel and refinery gas) 10.7%, natural gas (including gas derivatives) 11.2%, renewable fuels (including waste incineration) 2.4% and hydropower (including wind and geothermal power) 6.8% (Frees & Weidema, 1998).
The extraction, production and consumption of the fuel used during transportation were included in the study, but not the production of vehicles, buildings or infrastructure.

22: A brief description of the inventory data follows. A detailed description of the inventory data with original references can be found in Berlin (2001). The following ingredients and materials are required to produce 1 kg of Angsgärden cheese at the cheesemaking dairy: milk 10.1 kg, rennet 3 g, calcium chloride (100%) 1 g, salt- petre 0.65g salt 15 g, water 1.2kg, nitric acid (62%) 7.4g, sodium hydroxide (49%) 10.8g, plastics 9.6g and cardboard 25.6g.

The main ingredient in cheese is milk. The data used for milk production, i.e. the agricultural phase were taken from Cederberg's (1998) study of milk produc- tion. The activities included in Cederberg's study are illustrated in Fig. 3. The functional unit was 1000 kg energy-corrected milk (ECM) leaving the farm gate. ECM is a correction factor which considers both the fat and protein content of the milk. A conventional farm situated in the southwest of Sweden was the farm under study. The farm not only produced milk; meat was also produced at the same time. Therefore, an economical allocation was used where the profits from the produced products (meat and milk) were considered.

The rennet-producing company purchases stomachs from all over the world. The production of the stomachs was not included in the study, as they may be con- sidered as waste during milk and meat production. However, the transportation of the stomachs to the
Production of fertilisers, diesel, pesticides, seeds
Fig. 3. A flow diagram of activities included in the LCA of milk production (Cederberg & Mattsson, 2000).
rennet-producing company, the production of the rennet and the transportation of the rennet to the cheesemak- ing dairy were included.

Production of CaCl2 requires salt, lime and waste liquid from the production of dicalcium phosphate (DCP). The waste liquid from the production of DCP contains 13% CaCl2. This waste liquid is considered as a co-product and is used during manufacturing of CaCl2. An allocation based on weight between DCP and its waste liquid has been done. The inputs to DCP production are raw phosphate, limestone, hydrochloric acid (HCI) and energy. HCI is produced at the same factory as DCP and CaCl2. It is a co-product during the production of potassium sulphate (K2SO4). Allocation based on weight was made between K2SO4 and HCI.

The saltpetre (KNO3) used in the cheesemaking dairy was produced in Sweden using potassium chloride and nitric acid. The potassium chloride originated in Germany and the nitric acid was produced at the same factory as the saltpetre. During the production of saltpetre, hydrochloric acid was produced as a co- product. Therefore a weight allocation was performed between saltpetre and hydrochloric acid.

The salt used by the Falkenberg dairy was extracted in Denmark. The method used to produce the salt includes brine pumping, drying and purification. The water used at the dairy is ground water treated in a water treatment plant to the quality of drinking water. Energy and chemicals are required to disinfect the water, and energy is also used during its distribution.

The cleaning of dairy equipment requires both acidic and alkaline detergents, in this case, nitric acid and sodium hydroxide. The nitric acid was produced in Sweden from ammonia and air. The production of ammonia was assumed to take place in Russia, using natural gas and energy as inputs. Sodium hydroxide is produced in Sweden and the resources required are iron, limestone, water, sodium chloride, sand and energy.
The cheese was wrapped in a plastic coating at the dairy. Two different kinds of plastic foils both made of low-density polyethylene (LDPE) and polyamide (PA) were used.

In 1999 the cheese dairy of Arla Foods in Falkenberg, Sweden, used 254000 tonnes of incoming milk to produce 19700 tonnes of cheese, 3900 tonnes of milk powder, 52 200 tonnes of different whey concentrates and powder, and 13 000 tonnes of cream (Arla Foods AB, 2000). Economic allocation was performed, as described in Section 3.2. All wastewater effluent includ- ing water emissions from the cheesemaking dairy was treated in the municipal sewage treatment plant.
The starter culture was produced at the dairy and is included in the total figures for the cheesemaking dairy.
During transportation of the cheese from the cheese- making dairy to the retailer, a cardboard box of corrugated board was used as secondary packaging.

The corrugated board consists of kraftliner and semi- chemical fluting. The layers are glued together with cornstarch. The cornstarch was not included in this LCA as a very small amount was used. Energy requirements for forestry and hauling were taken into account in relation to the processing of wood.

At the retailer the cheese was stored in a cold room or a refrigerator, and electricity was required for cooling. Consumers purchasing the cheese used various modes of transportation, including cars. Swedish statistical data on the distance travelled for food purchasing and the modes of transportation used by consumers in Sweden were used. Because customers generally purchase other products at the same time as they purchase cheese, an economic allocation was performed between Angsgarden cheese and other food products purchased at the same time, using statistical data on the average purchase in a Swedish food store and the price of Angsgärden.

The energy requirements of the cheese once in a household were calculated based on the average refrigerator's electricity consumption, the space required for the cheese in the refrigerator, the consumption of hard and semi-hard cheese in Sweden, and the number of persons in an average household. It was assumed that every household always has a cheese in the refrigerator.

Waste generated at the household included both the plastic wrapping and some of the cheese (3%), but not the household wastewater (after consumption). The cardboard box used to deliver the cheese to the retailer was also treated as waste. Statistics show that 45% of the waste generated in Swedish households was incin- erated in 1998 (SEPA, 2002), and this figure will probably increase when a ban on using landfills to dispose of organic waste comes into effect in 2005 (Miljödepartementet, 2001). In this study all waste was considered to be incinerated except part of the card- board, which was assumed to be recycled. Energy was recovered during incineration and therefore a system expansion was done, as illustrated in Fig. 4. The system was compensated for the delivered energy by subtracting the resource use and emissions from alternative energy production. In Sweden, the recycling rate of corrugated board is 84% (Returwell, 2001), with the remainder being incinerated. Of the recycled corrugated board, 44% was assumed to replace new fibres while the remainder replaces recycled fibres (Ekvall, 2000). How- ever, the replacement of recycled fibre causes a surplus of recycled fibres on the market, and therefore the same amount of recycled fibre is assumed to leave the market as waste. Fig. 5 illustrates the system expansion for corrugated board.

The sources of energy used in this LCA are natural gas, oil and electricity. The Swedish mixture of energy sources for electricity production was used when the requirement for electricity was located in Sweden, and

A system expansion was performed (the bold part of the figure) as the recycled corrugated board replaces paper produced from virgin material as well as paper produced from already recycled material. The effect of this is a decreased production of paper from virgin material and the replaced recycled paper is incinerated.

The following ingredients and materials are required to produce 1 kg of Angsgärden cheese at the cheesemaking dairy: milk 10.1 kg, rennet 3 g, calcium chloride (100%) 1 g, salt- petre 0.65g salt 15 g, water 1.2kg, nitric acid (62%) 7.4g, sodium hydroxide (49%) 10.8g, plastics 9.6g and cardboard 25.6g.

The main ingredient in cheese is milk. The data used for milk production, i.e. the agricultural phase were taken from Cederberg's (1998) study of milk produc- tion. The activities included in Cederberg's study are illustrated in Fig. 3. The functional unit was 1000 kg energy-corrected milk (ECM) leaving the farm gate. ECM is a correction factor which considers both the fat and protein content of the milk. A conventional farm situated in the southwest of Sweden was the farm under study. The farm not only produced milk; meat was also produced at the same time. Therefore, an economical allocation was used where the profits from the produced products (meat and milk) were considered.

The rennet-producing company purchases stomachs from all over the world. The production of the stomachs was not included in the study, as they may be con- sidered as waste during milk and meat production. However, the transportation of the stomachs to the
Production of fertilisers, diesel, pesticides, seeds
Fig. 3. A flow diagram of activities included in the LCA of milk production (Cederberg & Mattsson, 2000).
rennet-producing company, the production of the rennet and the transportation of the rennet to the cheesemak- ing dairy were included.

Production of CaCl2 requires salt, lime and waste liquid from the production of dicalcium phosphate (DCP). The waste liquid from the production of DCP contains 13% CaCl2. This waste liquid is considered as a co-product and is used during manufacturing of CaCl2. An allocation based on weight between DCP and its waste liquid has been done. The inputs to DCP production are raw phosphate, limestone, hydrochloric acid (HCI) and energy. HCI is produced at the same factory as DCP and CaCl2. It is a co-product during the production of potassium sulphate (K2SO4). Allocation based on weight was made between K2SO4 and HCI.

The saltpetre (KNO3) used in the cheesemaking dairy was produced in Sweden using potassium chloride and nitric acid. The potassium chloride originated in Germany and the nitric acid was produced at the same factory as the saltpetre. During the production of saltpetre, hydrochloric acid was produced as a co- product. Therefore a weight allocation was performed between saltpetre and hydrochloric acid.

The salt used by the Falkenberg dairy was extracted in Denmark. The method used to produce the salt includes brine pumping, drying and purification. The water used at the dairy is ground water treated in a water treatment plant to the quality of drinking water. Energy and chemicals are required to disinfect the water, and energy is also used during its distribution.

The cleaning of dairy equipment requires both acidic and alkaline detergents, in this case, nitric acid and sodium hydroxide. The nitric acid was produced in Sweden from ammonia and air. The production of ammonia was assumed to take place in Russia, using natural gas and energy as inputs. Sodium hydroxide is produced in Sweden and the resources required are iron, limestone, water, sodium chloride, sand and energy.
The cheese was wrapped in a plastic coating at the dairy. Two different kinds of plastic foils both made of low-density polyethylene (LDPE) and polyamide (PA) were used.

In 1999 the cheese dairy of Arla Foods in Falkenberg, Sweden, used 254000 tonnes of incoming milk to produce 19700 tonnes of cheese, 3900 tonnes of milk powder, 52 200 tonnes of different whey concentrates and powder, and 13 000 tonnes of cream (Arla Foods AB, 2000). Economic allocation was performed, as described in Section 3.2. All wastewater effluent includ- ing water emissions from the cheesemaking dairy was treated in the municipal sewage treatment plant.
The starter culture was produced at the dairy and is included in the total figures for the cheesemaking dairy.
During transportation of the cheese from the cheese- making dairy to the retailer, a cardboard box of corrugated board was used as secondary packaging.

The corrugated board consists of kraftliner and semi- chemical fluting. The layers are glued together with cornstarch. The cornstarch was not included in this LCA as a very small amount was used. Energy requirements for forestry and hauling were taken into account in relation to the processing of wood.

At the retailer the cheese was stored in a cold room or a refrigerator, and electricity was required for cooling. Consumers purchasing the cheese used various modes of transportation, including cars. Swedish statistical data on the distance travelled for food purchasing and the modes of transportation used by consumers in Sweden were used. Because customers generally purchase other products at the same time as they purchase cheese, an economic allocation was performed between Angsgarden cheese and other food products purchased at the same time, using statistical data on the average purchase in a Swedish food store and the price of Angsgärden.

The energy requirements of the cheese once in a household were calculated based on the average refrigerator's electricity consumption, the space required for the cheese in the refrigerator, the consumption of hard and semi-hard cheese in Sweden, and the number of persons in an average household. It was assumed that every household always has a cheese in the refrigerator.

Waste generated at the household included both the plastic wrapping and some of the cheese (3%), but not the household wastewater (after consumption). The cardboard box used to deliver the cheese to the retailer was also treated as waste. Statistics show that 45% of the waste generated in Swedish households was incin- erated in 1998 (SEPA, 2002), and this figure will probably increase when a ban on using landfills to dispose of organic waste comes into effect in 2005 (Miljödepartementet, 2001). In this study all waste was considered to be incinerated except part of the card- board, which was assumed to be recycled. Energy was recovered during incineration and therefore a system expansion was done, as illustrated in Fig. 4. The system was compensated for the delivered energy by subtracting the resource use and emissions from alternative energy production. How- ever, the replacement of recycled fibre causes a surplus of recycled fibres on the market, and therefore the same amount of recycled fibre is assumed to leave the market as waste. Fig. 5 illustrates the system expansion for corrugated board.

31: The most important resource in food production is land, which is the base of production. The area of farmland required to produce the roughage, grain and concentrate feed used at the dairy farm was 14 2 per year and kg Angsgärden.

Dairy farming was the activity that required the most of material resources. The materials required in large quantities were lime, potassium and phosphorus. The life cycle of 1 kg Angsgarden required 26.5 g limestone, 21.5 g potassium and 20.4g phosphorus. Lime was used
as soil improver on the farm and ends up in concentrate feed used at the dairy farm. The phosphorus and potassium were used in synthetic fertiliser applied to crops used in concentrate feed.
32: Energy requirements are included in other impact categories with the emissions released during the extracting and production of energy. However, to increase our understanding of energy consumption, the amount and sources of energy required for each activity are presented separately.

Producing the ingredients, detergents, plastic and cardboard, making the cheese, refrigerating the cheese at the retailer and in the household, managing the waste, and transporting all products at each stage in the life cycle required a total of 9.1 MJ of electricity and 30 MJ
of fuels (fossil, renewable and unspecified) (Table 2). The resources used in largest quantity to meet the energy requirements were crude oil 0.44kg, natural gas 0.29 kg and hard coal 0.05 kg.

The largest consumption of energy took place during the production of milk at the farm. The most common source of energy at the farm was fossil fuel, but other sources were also used. Natural gas used for synthetic fertiliser production represented 36% of the fossil fuel used at the farm. The remainder of the fossil fuel was diesel to power tractors and agricultural equipment.

38: The emissions that contributed most to acidification were NH3, NOx and SO2. The equivalency factors used (SO2 equivalents) are taken from the CML Guide (Heijungs et al., 1992). The total contribution to acidification was 136g SO2 equivalents per kg cheese (Table 3). Milk production was clearly the dominant contributor, with 135 g SO2 equivalents per FU. The cheesemaking dairy contributed 0.8 SO2 equivalents, and the plastic production contributed 0.3g SO2 equivalents per 1kg cheese (Table 3). The ammonia emitted at the milk-producing farm contributed 119 g SO2 equivalents per FU, almost exclusively through ammonia evaporation from farmyard manure (Ceder- berg, 1998). The NOx and SO2 emitted during milk production originated from the use of fossil fuels. The transportation of the cheese to the retailer was the largest source of emitted NOx from the dairy. During plastic production, NOx originating from the produc- tion of LDPE and PA contributed most to acidification.
39: This potential impact of this cheese on eutrophication was 2.13kg O2 equivalents per FU (Table 3). The equivalency factors used (O2 equivalents) in this calculation were taken from the Nordic Guidelines on Life-Cycle Assessment (Lindfors et al., 1995). Milk production was the largest source of eutrophication with 2.12kg O2 per 1 kg cheese (Table 3). Emissions of ammonia and nitrate contributed the most to eutrophi- cation during milk production; the ammonia evaporated from farmyard manure and the nitrate leached from the soil. The degree of leaching of nitrate depends on how and when manure is spread on the fields, as well as on the management of the soil and the amount of fertilisers used.

The dairy was the second largest source with 0.01 kg O2 per FU (Table 3), originating mainly in emissions of NOx to air and of phosphorus and nitrogen to water. The largest source of NOx from the activity of the dairy was the transport of the cheese from the dairy to the retailer. The cheesemaking process was the source of the emitted phosphorus and nitrogen. The sources for the phosphorus and nitrogen included the use of

detergents such as phosphoric acid and nitric acid for cleaning the moulds. The cleaning process produced an effluent containing water and milk, and it was the milk in the effluent that was responsible for most of the emitted phosphorus and nitrogen.

42: Leakage of cooling media contributes to the depletion of the ozone layer. However, as data on the leakage were uncertain, no quantitative results can be given. The activities that used cooling equipment were the dairy farm, the rennet-producing company, the cheesemaking dairy, the retailer, the household and cold storage transportation. Given the number of activities using cooling equipment, some leakage of cooling media must occur in the life cycle of cheese. But it is also important to bear in mind that not all cooling media deplete ozone-for example, R134a and R404a do not con- tribute to ozone depletion (Lorentzon et al., 1997). However, leakage of R134a does contribute to global warming. The retailers under study used R134a, R404a and R22 (Carlson & Sonesson, 2000). Ammonia is another cooling medium with no ozone depletion potential. It was used as a cooling medium by the cheesemaking dairy (Karlsson, 2001). However, leakage of ammonia does contribute to eutrophication and acidification.
43: The toxic elements in this study were heavy metals and pesticides. The heavy metals that are relevant from an environmental point of view (SEPA, 2001) are listed in Table 4 together with the amounts emitted. The

0: <title>Heavy metal emissions during the life cycle of the cheese </title>

production of diesel and heavy fuel oil was the largest source of heavy metal emissions, and affected every activity that involved transportation, in other words, all activities except water production. As milk production and cheesemaking included much transportation, these activities emitted the most heavy metals.

The use of commercial fertilisers is also a source of heavy metals. For example, phosphorus fertilisers used on arable land contain As, Cd and Zn. During the production of phosphorus fertilisers, fluorides are emitted as a consequence of the presence of fluorides in raw phosphate (Andersson, 1998). The dairy farm under study (like the majority of Swedish dairy farms) did not use commercial P-fertilisers since manure supplied the phosphorus needed. However, the farms that grew the crops for concentrate feed production used commercial fertilisers (Cederberg & Mattsson, 2000). As the arable farms were regarded as falling within the activity of milk production in this study, the use of commercial fertilisers was another source of heavy metals.

The use of pesticides contributes to both eco- and human toxicity. Herbicides, insecticides and fungicides were used in cultivation. No further analysis of pesticide use was performed in this study, but more information can be found in Cederberg et al. (2000).

The use of detergents at the cheesemaking dairy could have been a source of toxic compounds. However, as the amount of detergents not recommended by the National Chemicals Inspectorate in Sweden was less than 1% of the total amount of chemicals used at Arla Foods Falkenberg cheesemaking dairy, the possible contribu- tion of detergents to toxicity was ignored.

3: This LCA of semi-hard cheese includes the produc- tion of ingredients, detergents, plastic, cardboard; the cheesemaking process at the dairy; the retailer, the household; and waste management, as well as energy production and transports. All activities included contribute to the environmental impact. By far the largest contributor to the environmental impact is the production of milk. As cheese consists mainly of milk, this is hardly surprising. Another significant contributor to the environmental impact is the cheesemaking dairy. The use of electricity at the retailer and the production
of plastic are also activities with substantial environ- mental impact.

Similar results have been found in other studies. In a study of the life cycle of Camembert, Bernhard and Moos (1998) came to the conclusion that the agricultur- al component contributed most to the environmental impact, but that consumption and industrial production also had significant effects. In a study of the life cycle of milk powder, the agricultural component was again identified as the main source of environmental impacts, followed by industrial processing (Blonk et al., 1997). Hogaas Eide (2002a), too, identified the agricultural component as having the greatest environmental impact when consumer milk was studied.

In several studies of dairy products, only part of the life cycle of the product has been considered. Masoni et al. (1998) excluded the agricultural component in their LCA of butter, and concluded that the manufac- turing stage contributed most to the environmental impact. A study of yoghurt by Meier-Ploeger and Fuchs (1996) from which the retailer, household, and waste management were excluded concluded that the agricul- tural component consumed 20% of the total energy used in the whole life cycle. In the present study, which includes those areas, the corresponding figure for the energy requirements of the agricultural component is 73%. (However, it must be pointed out that that figure refers to the energy required to produce 10.1 kg of milk, as that was the amount required to produce 1 kg of Angsgärden.) The cumulative effect of all these studies is to suggest that the agricultural component and the dairy have the greatest environment irrespective of the type of milk product under study.

5: In order to make substantial improvements in the environmental performance of cheese production, it is necessary to address the activities that contribute most to the environmental impact.

From the dairy's perspective, an important improve- ment would be to decrease the amount of milk required to produce cheese. Identifying and minimising the losses of milk during the production of cheese would decrease the consumption of milk without affecting the final product. There are methods to increase the yield of cheese during the cheesemaking process, but all have the drawback of affecting the quality of the cheese. These
methods include increasing the water and salt content of the cheese, and retaining more whey protein in the curd (Bertelsen, Sven-Nilsson, & Willart, 1983; Johansson, 2001).

Increasing the protein content in the incoming milk also gives a better yield of cheese (Johansson, 2001). The protein content of milk depends on such factors as the breed of cattle and the fodder used. However, the choice of fodder will affect the environmental impact of farming, and hence it is not possible to predict how changing this might affect the outcome of the entire system.

Improving farming practices with the environment in mind could substantially improve the results of this study. Cederberg (1998) and Cederberg and Bergström (1999) suggest possible ways of improving milk production. Consumers, too, could contribute by minimising wastage of cheese in the household and reducing car transportation from the retailer to the household.

7: Most of the data in this study were derived from the literature, which met the requirements for identification of resources and emissions connected with the life cycle of semi-hard cheese. However, site-specific data were also used.

The quality of the data is most important in regard to those activities that have the greatest environmental impact, namely milk production, cheesemaking, the production of plastic, and retailing. Although it would have been preferable to be able to use average data on milk production, no such data were available and SO specific data have been used. The data for milk production came from one conventional farm situated in the same area as the cheesemaking dairy. To what extent can this farm be considered representative of the average Swedish dairy farm? Cederberg et al. (1999) studied the flow of nutrients and land use for milk production on 14 conventional farms in the south of Sweden. Comparing the result of their study with the data for milk production in this study suggests that the parameters for total land use per kg milk, nitrogen surplus and phosphorus surplus are representative of a farm in the south of Sweden.

The data from the cheesemaking dairy were also site specific. As Arla Foods Falkenberg is the largest producer of cheese in Sweden, the data were considered to be sufficiently reliable to be used for general purposes. Site-specific data were also used for the sewage treatment plant where the wastewater effluent from the cheesemaking dairy was treated. The treatment capacity at the treatment plant that the Falkenberg dairy uses is BOD 99%, COD 96%, tot-P 97%, and tot-N 87% (Petersson, 2002). For comparison, the treatment

capacity figures for the sewage treatment plant in Göteborg, Sweden, are BOD 96%, COD 89%, tot-P 91% and tot-N 58% (GRYAAB, 2001). These figures indicate that there are differences in sewage treatment capacity between plants.

The data used for the production of plastic were average data, which is an advantage since the purpose of the study was to investigate the general environmental consequences of the life cycle of cheese. However, some data on plastic production dated from 1993.

Three retailers provided the data for the energy requirements of retailer activity. The average energy requirement was used, although the differences in the energy requirements were substantial, varying from 346 to 570 and 573 kWh m (Carlson & Sonesson, 2000). It was decided that these data were acceptable because the study was a recent one and the retailers were situated in Sweden.

The data used should preferably be as recent as possible. The oldest data used in this study dated from 1992. However, that data concerned the energy require- ments of household refrigerators, which made a very minor contribution to the total environmental impact. Some of the data used in regard to water production were also rather old (dating from 1993) but, as with the household data, the water data made a very minor contribution to the total environmental impact.

Several allocations and assumptions had to be made regarding transportation from the retailer to the house- hold, as well as regarding use within the household. For example, the assumption that 3% of the cheese is wasted is very uncertain. Angsgärden cheese is wrapped in plastic and has therefore not any hard edges; SO in fact the whole cheese can be eaten. But, it is hard to slice the cheese when it is only a small part left and therefore the last part is often considered as waste by the consumer. Improper storage is another example of source of waste in the household. Yet this assumption of 3% affects the results not only for the household, but also for transport from the household to the waste management facility, and also the incineration at the waste management activity. Consequently the confidence of the results relating to those activities is rather low. While it might have been possible to conduct a more detailed study of those activities with uncertain assumptions and alloca- tions, the fact that these activities were shown to make a low contribution to the total environmental impact of the life cycle of cheese meant that further research in these areas was considered unnecessary in the present study.

However, that data concerned the energy require- ments of household refrigerators, which made a very minor contribution to the total environmental impact. Some of the data used in regard to water production were also rather old (dating from 1993) but, as with the household data, the water data made a very minor contribution to the total environmental impact.

Several allocations and assumptions had to be made regarding transportation from the retailer to the house- hold, as well as regarding use within the household. For example, the assumption that 3% of the cheese is wasted is very uncertain. Angsgärden cheese is wrapped in plastic and has therefore not any hard edges; SO in fact the whole cheese can be eaten. But, it is hard to slice the cheese when it is only a small part left and therefore the last part is often considered as waste by the consumer. Improper storage is another example of source of waste in the household. Yet this assumption of 3% affects the results not only for the household, but also for transport from the household to the waste management facility, and also the incineration at the waste management activity. Consequently the confidence of the results relating to those activities is rather low. While it might have been possible to conduct a more detailed study of those activities with uncertain assumptions and alloca- tions, the fact that these activities were shown to make a low contribution to the total environmental impact of the life cycle of cheese meant that further research in these areas was considered unnecessary in the present study.

The quality of the data is most important in regard to those activities that have the greatest environmental impact, namely milk production, cheesemaking, the production of plastic, and retailing. Although it would have been preferable to be able to use average data on milk production, no such data were available and SO specific data have been used. The data for milk production came from one conventional farm situated in the same area as the cheesemaking dairy. To what extent can this farm be considered representative of the average Swedish dairy farm? Cederberg et al. (1999) studied the flow of nutrients and land use for milk production on 14 conventional farms in the south of Sweden. Comparing the result of their study with the data for milk production in this study suggests that the parameters for total land use per kg milk, nitrogen surplus and phosphorus surplus are representative of a farm in the south of Sweden.

The data from the cheesemaking dairy were also site specific. As Arla Foods Falkenberg is the largest producer of cheese in Sweden, the data were considered to be sufficiently reliable to be used for general purposes. Site-specific data were also used for the sewage treatment plant where the wastewater effluent from the cheesemaking dairy was treated. The treatment capacity at the treatment plant that the Falkenberg dairy uses is BOD 99%, COD 96%, tot-P 97%, and tot-N 87% (Petersson, 2002). For comparison, the treatment

capacity figures for the sewage treatment plant in Göteborg, Sweden, are BOD 96%, COD 89%, tot-P 91% and tot-N 58% (GRYAAB, 2001). These figures indicate that there are differences in sewage treatment capacity between plants.

The data used for the production of plastic were average data, which is an advantage since the purpose of the study was to investigate the general environmental consequences of the life cycle of cheese. However, some data on plastic production dated from 1993.

Three retailers provided the data for the energy requirements of retailer activity. The average energy requirement was used, although the differences in the energy requirements were substantial, varying from 346 to 570 and 573 kWh m (Carlson & Sonesson, 2000). It was decided that these data were acceptable because the study was a recent one and the retailers were situated in Sweden.

11: <header>5.3. Sensitivity to system boundaries and allocation choices </header>
The upper boundary of this study was the acquisition of raw materials for most of the ingredients and energy

carriers. The cattle involved in the activity of rennet production were not included because rennet makes only a small contribution to the environmental impact of the life cycle of cheese. Thus the agricultural component of the production of the stomachs would not affect the total result.

The treatment of household wastewater (after con- sumption) was not included in the study. It is not general practice to include that component in food LCA studies, but this is an area of potential improvement both for food LCAs in general and for this particular study.

Economical allocation was used between meat and milk in the agricultural phase. The difficulties to divide the environmental burden between milk, meat and surplus calves have been discussed by Cederberg (2002). Cederberg and Stadig (2001) did a comparison of avoiding allocation with system expansion with three allocation methods. They came to the conclusion that system expansion was preferable. However, the system must be expanded with a complete LCA of beef production, which was considered to be outside the goal of this study of cheese. Economical allocation between milk and meat favours the product meat (Cederberg & Stadig, 2001). Considering a somewhat lower allocation factor for milk for this study would not affect the result as it was clear that the production of milk at the farm contributed most to the environmental impact in all categories (Fig. 6).

Hogaas Eide and Olsson (1998) did a comparison of two different approaches to the inventory analysis of dairies, the simplified method and the detailed method. The simplified method considers the total environmental load from a dairy and then allocates the load to the product under study. The environmental load for each process step during dairy production was considered in the detailed method. They conclude that the choice of inventory method depends on the purpose of the LCA study. For identification of the activities that contribute most to the environmental burden from the total life cycle, the simplified method is preferable. With the detailed method it is possible to identify the environ- mental burden of each process step within the dairy. As the purpose of this study was similar to that of the simplified method, this was used. The simplified method includes allocation. There are several bases that can be used for allocation in dairies. In Hogaas Eide (2002b) a review of the bases used for dairy products can be found. The allocation technique used in this study at the cheesemaking dairy was economical. This allocation method was also used by Nilsson and Lorentzon (2000) in their environmental assessment of dairies. It is a commonly used method in LCA of food products. It is important to bear in mind that the allocation method affects the result. Weidema, Pedersen and Drivsholm (1995) suggest a quick worst-case approach that involves

allocating all environmental effects to the product under study. Were that kind of allocation technique to be used, the environmental impact of the activities of milk production and the cheesemaking dairy would have been even worser than in this study, which allocated only 67.8% of milk production and of the activities of the cheesemaking dairy to the cheese. Teulon (1993) also discusses the problem of co-products in the field of food LCA and uses physical correlation in her example of the partitioning of cheese and whey on the basis of fat content. When she compared the results with those obtained from economic allocation, they were very similar. It thus appears that the choice of allocation method for the cheesemaking dairy in this study was of minor importance in identifying the activities that contributed most to the environmental impact of the life cycle of cheese.

The choice of allocation method affects the result of the study and for prospective LCA studies that does not get as clear a result as that in this study, I would recommend to make quantitative calculations of the result impact of allocation choices.

15: Although cheese is a dairy product which is highly industrially processed, the conclusion from this study is that the activity that contributes the most to the environmental impact is the agricultural phase, that is, milk production. This is in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products. Other activities that have important environmental impacts include cheesemaking at the dairy, the production of plastic, and retailing. Improving these activities is the most efficient way of improving the overall environmental impact of the life cycle of cheese. Decreasing the wastage of milk and cheese throughout the life cycle should be the first step towards improvement, without affecting the quality of the cheese. From a dairy perspective, the most effective action would be to decrease the requirement for milk in the cheese, as this would mean that less milk would have to be produced at the farm. However, there is the risk that the quality of the cheese might change when the cheesemaking process is changed.
